meta.title: Swire RGM Enterprise Assessment
meta.assessmentFocus: Core maturity assessment with dashboard outputs by pillar, stage, and enabler.

pillars.label: Pricing
pillars.description: Price realization, architecture, ex-factory discipline, P&L impact, and long-term price direction.

pillars.label: OBPPC
pillars.description: Revenue architecture across occasions, brands, packs, price points, and channels.

pillars.label: Promotion Spend
pillars.description: Promotion role, spend efficiency, guardrails, ROI discipline, and reallocation logic.

pillars.label: DFR / Trade Investment
pillars.description: Commercial terms, funding logic, gross-to-net discipline, and customer investment structure.

stages.label: 1. Opportunity Understanding
stages.description: Whether the market can diagnose value pools, market realities, and performance gaps with fact-based clarity.

stages.label: 2. Strategy Design
stages.description: Whether the market can convert insight into a coherent pillar strategy and clear prioritization.

stages.label: 3. Policy Translation
stages.description: Whether strategy is translated into rules, guardrails, commercial policy, and operating logic.

stages.label: 4. Execution Management
stages.description: Whether the market manages implementation through tools, data, routines, and closed-loop review.

stages.label: 5. Capability & Forward Planning
stages.description: Whether the market can sustain improvement through people, governance, roadmap thinking, and future scenario planning.

enablers.label: Governance
enablers.description: Decision rights, review cadence, escalation, KPI reviews, and cross-functional working routines.

enablers.label: Digital / Data / Tools
enablers.description: Dashboards, tool adoption, data availability, analytics maturity, and workflow support.

enablers.label: People & Capability Development
enablers.description: Role clarity, team capability, RGM literacy, leadership profile, training, and certification.

enablers.label: Forward-looking
enablers.description: Long-term roadmap thinking, scenario planning, future profit pools, and reallocation discipline.

questions.text: Pricing decisions are based on more than inflation and COGS; they incorporate competition, consumer willingness to pay, and strategic price positioning.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide a current strategy deck, roadmap, architecture, or decision memo.

anchors.1: No reliable capability exists to set pricing using more than inflation and COGS. Decision support around competition, willingness-to-pay, and strategic price positioning inputs is absent, too weak, or not decision-grade.
anchors.2: Basic capability exists to set pricing using more than inflation and COGS, but decision support around competition, willingness-to-pay, and strategic price positioning inputs is limited in scope, mostly manual, or used only occasionally.
anchors.3: A workable capability exists to set pricing using more than inflation and COGS for major decisions, and decision support around competition, willingness-to-pay, and strategic price positioning inputs provides useful directional input, but depth and consistency remain limited.
anchors.4: A strong capability exists to set pricing using more than inflation and COGS. Decision support around competition, willingness-to-pay, and strategic price positioning inputs is robust, regularly updated, and actively used in decision making.
anchors.5: A leading capability exists to set pricing using more than inflation and COGS. Decision support around competition, willingness-to-pay, and strategic price positioning inputs is integrated, predictive where relevant, and used to continuously improve strategic and financial decisions.

questions.text: The market uses elasticity, cross-elasticity, switching behavior, or similar analytical methods to support pricing decisions.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide dashboard, model output, analysis file, KPI tracker, or tool screenshot.

anchors.1: No reliable capability exists to use elasticity and switching analysis in pricing decisions. Decision support around direct elasticity, cross-elasticity, switching behavior, and interpretation is absent, too weak, or not decision-grade.
anchors.2: Basic capability exists to use elasticity and switching analysis in pricing decisions, but decision support around direct elasticity, cross-elasticity, switching behavior, and interpretation is limited in scope, mostly manual, or used only occasionally.
anchors.3: A workable capability exists to use elasticity and switching analysis in pricing decisions for major decisions, and decision support around direct elasticity, cross-elasticity, switching behavior, and interpretation provides useful directional input, but depth and consistency remain limited.
anchors.4: A strong capability exists to use elasticity and switching analysis in pricing decisions. Decision support around direct elasticity, cross-elasticity, switching behavior, and interpretation is robust, regularly updated, and actively used in decision making.
anchors.5: A leading capability exists to use elasticity and switching analysis in pricing decisions. Decision support around direct elasticity, cross-elasticity, switching behavior, and interpretation is integrated, predictive where relevant, and used to continuously improve strategic and financial decisions.

questions.text: The market can estimate the likely impact of pricing decisions on volume, NSR, gross profit, and profit pool at SKU or channel level.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide dashboard, model output, analysis file, KPI tracker, or tool screenshot.

anchors.1: No reliable capability exists to estimate the financial impact of price decisions. Decision support around volume, NSR, gross profit, and profit-pool effects by SKU or channel is absent, too weak, or not decision-grade.
anchors.2: Basic capability exists to estimate the financial impact of price decisions, but decision support around volume, NSR, gross profit, and profit-pool effects by SKU or channel is limited in scope, mostly manual, or used only occasionally.
anchors.3: A workable capability exists to estimate the financial impact of price decisions for major decisions, and decision support around volume, NSR, gross profit, and profit-pool effects by SKU or channel provides useful directional input, but depth and consistency remain limited.
anchors.4: A strong capability exists to estimate the financial impact of price decisions. Decision support around volume, NSR, gross profit, and profit-pool effects by SKU or channel is robust, regularly updated, and actively used in decision making.
anchors.5: A leading capability exists to estimate the financial impact of price decisions. Decision support around volume, NSR, gross profit, and profit-pool effects by SKU or channel is integrated, predictive where relevant, and used to continuously improve strategic and financial decisions.

questions.text: The market has a clear pricing strategy by brand, pack, and channel that is consistent with brand positioning and commercial objectives.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide a current strategy deck, roadmap, architecture, or decision memo.

anchors.1: No clear brand-, pack-, and channel-level pricing strategy is in place. The business lacks structure around brand roles, channel differentiation, and competitive price gaps, so choices remain reactive and weakly aligned to strategy.
anchors.2: A basic brand-, pack-, and channel-level pricing strategy is in place in selected parts of the business, but the treatment of brand roles, channel differentiation, and competitive price gaps is incomplete, outdated, or inconsistently applied.
anchors.3: A defined brand-, pack-, and channel-level pricing strategy is in place for major brands, channels, customers, or packs, and brand roles, channel differentiation, and competitive price gaps inform key decisions, but coverage and rigor remain uneven.
anchors.4: A strong brand-, pack-, and channel-level pricing strategy is documented and regularly used. Brand roles, channel differentiation, and competitive price gaps are clearly translated into decisions through an insight-led process.
anchors.5: A leading brand-, pack-, and channel-level pricing strategy is dynamic and forward-looking. Brand roles, channel differentiation, and competitive price gaps are continuously refreshed and actively shape both current choices and medium- to long-term value creation.

questions.text: The market has a clearly defined medium- to long-term price ladder or price curve, including affordability, mainstream, and premium roles where relevant.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide a current strategy deck, roadmap, architecture, or decision memo.

anchors.1: No meaningful forward-looking view exists for price ladder or long-term price curve. Affordability, mainstream, premium roles, and ladder coherence are not used to shape medium- to long-term choices.
anchors.2: An initial forward-looking view exists for price ladder or long-term price curve, but affordability, mainstream, premium roles, and ladder coherence are high level, weakly evidenced, or not yet linked to action.
anchors.3: A workable forward-looking view exists for price ladder or long-term price curve. Affordability, mainstream, premium roles, and ladder coherence inform planning for major priorities, but scenario depth and strategic linkage are limited.
anchors.4: A strong forward-looking view exists for price ladder or long-term price curve. Affordability, mainstream, premium roles, and ladder coherence are evidence-based and actively shape medium-term planning and investment choices.
anchors.5: A leading forward-looking view exists for price ladder or long-term price curve. Affordability, mainstream, premium roles, and ladder coherence are scenario-tested, dynamic, and directly shape current decisions as well as 3–5 year direction.

questions.text: Ex-factory prices are centrally governed with clear approval rules, documented logic, and integration with promotion and trade investment management.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide SOP, governance routine, review deck, workflow, or implementation example.

anchors.1: No reliable process exists for ex-factory pricing governance. The business handles approval rules, documented logic, and linkage to promotions and trade investment ad hoc, weakly controls them, or does not follow through.
anchors.2: A basic process exists for ex-factory pricing governance, but the management of approval rules, documented logic, and linkage to promotions and trade investment is manual, inconsistent, or applied only in selected cases.
anchors.3: A defined process exists for ex-factory pricing governance in major cases, and approval rules, documented logic, and linkage to promotions and trade investment are reviewed periodically, but discipline and closed-loop follow-through are uneven.
anchors.4: A robust process exists for ex-factory pricing governance. Approval rules, documented logic, and linkage to promotions and trade investment are reviewed regularly, exceptions are managed, and actions are followed through.
anchors.5: A leading process exists for ex-factory pricing governance. Approval rules, documented logic, and linkage to promotions and trade investment are tightly governed, rapidly escalated when needed, and continuously optimized through closed-loop management.

questions.text: There is a regular process to review realized prices, net prices, and compliance with target price corridors.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide SOP, governance routine, review deck, workflow, or implementation example.

anchors.1: No reliable process exists for review realized price, net price, and corridor compliance. The business handles price realization, corridor deviations, and corrective actions ad hoc, weakly controls them, or does not follow through.
anchors.2: A basic process exists for review realized price, net price, and corridor compliance, but the management of price realization, corridor deviations, and corrective actions is manual, inconsistent, or applied only in selected cases.
anchors.3: A defined process exists for review realized price, net price, and corridor compliance in major cases, and price realization, corridor deviations, and corrective actions are reviewed periodically, but discipline and closed-loop follow-through are uneven.
anchors.4: A robust process exists for review realized price, net price, and corridor compliance. Price realization, corridor deviations, and corrective actions are reviewed regularly, exceptions are managed, and actions are followed through.
anchors.5: A leading process exists for review realized price, net price, and corridor compliance. Price realization, corridor deviations, and corrective actions are tightly governed, rapidly escalated when needed, and continuously optimized through closed-loop management.

questions.text: Pricing decisions are translated into execution plans and tracked until implementation issues are resolved.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide SOP, governance routine, review deck, workflow, or implementation example.

anchors.1: No reliable process exists for translate pricing decisions into execution. The business handles owners, communication, implementation milestones, and issue resolution ad hoc, weakly controls them, or does not follow through.
anchors.2: A basic process exists for translate pricing decisions into execution, but the management of owners, communication, implementation milestones, and issue resolution is manual, inconsistent, or applied only in selected cases.
anchors.3: A defined process exists for translate pricing decisions into execution in major cases, and owners, communication, implementation milestones, and issue resolution are reviewed periodically, but discipline and closed-loop follow-through are uneven.
anchors.4: A robust process exists for translate pricing decisions into execution. Owners, communication, implementation milestones, and issue resolution are reviewed regularly, exceptions are managed, and actions are followed through.
anchors.5: A leading process exists for translate pricing decisions into execution. Owners, communication, implementation milestones, and issue resolution are tightly governed, rapidly escalated when needed, and continuously optimized through closed-loop management.

questions.text: Pricing dashboards or tools are used regularly to monitor execution and identify deviations from strategy.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide dashboard, model output, analysis file, KPI tracker, or tool screenshot.

anchors.1: No effective tools are used for pricing execution monitoring. Visibility into pricing exceptions, compliance gaps, and deviations from strategy is unreliable, late, or absent.
anchors.2: Basic tools exist for pricing execution monitoring, but pricing exceptions, compliance gaps, and deviations from strategy are tracked manually, inconsistently, or only for selected items.
anchors.3: Useful tools support pricing execution monitoring for major brands, channels, customers, or packs, and pricing exceptions, compliance gaps, and deviations from strategy are tracked regularly, but insight-to-action remains uneven.
anchors.4: Strong tools support pricing execution monitoring. Pricing exceptions, compliance gaps, and deviations from strategy are monitored through regular dashboards or alerts and are actively used to manage performance.
anchors.5: Leading tools support pricing execution monitoring. Pricing exceptions, compliance gaps, and deviations from strategy are automated, timely, and embedded in workflows to enable rapid intervention and ongoing optimization.

questions.text: Pricing decisions are reviewed through a formal cross-functional process involving RGM, Sales, Finance, and relevant commercial stakeholders.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide meeting cadence, RACI, forum minutes, or cross-functional decision example.

anchors.1: No clear cross-functional governance exists for cross-functional pricing decision making. Management of RGM, Sales, Finance, and commercial stakeholder involvement depends largely on individual relationships or single-function decisions.
anchors.2: Basic cross-functional governance exists for cross-functional pricing decision making, but management of RGM, Sales, Finance, and commercial stakeholder involvement is irregular, narrow in participation, or weak in decision authority.
anchors.3: A defined governance process exists for cross-functional pricing decision making, and RGM, Sales, Finance, and commercial stakeholder involvement are discussed in major decisions, but participation and follow-through are uneven.
anchors.4: Strong cross-functional governance exists for cross-functional pricing decision making. Rgm, sales, finance, and commercial stakeholder involvement are reviewed through clear forums, ownership, and escalation routes.
anchors.5: Leading cross-functional governance exists for cross-functional pricing decision making. Rgm, sales, finance, and commercial stakeholder involvement are managed through high-quality dialogue, clear trade-offs, and rapid aligned decisions.

questions.text: Pricing-related market insights and learning are systematically shared with regional or central RGM teams.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide meeting cadence, RACI, forum minutes, or cross-functional decision example.

anchors.1: No systematic sharing exists for sharing pricing insight with regional or central RGM teams. Market learning, implications, and recommended actions are retained locally, fragmented, or shared only on request.
anchors.2: Basic sharing exists for sharing pricing insight with regional or central RGM teams, but market learning, implications, and recommended actions are shared irregularly, at a high level, or without enough context to drive action.
anchors.3: A defined sharing routine exists for sharing pricing insight with regional or central RGM teams, and market learning, implications, and recommended actions are circulated on important topics, but sharing is not yet proactive or consistent.
anchors.4: Strong sharing exists for sharing pricing insight with regional or central RGM teams. Market learning, implications, and recommended actions are communicated regularly with enough context to support alignment and action.
anchors.5: Leading sharing exists for sharing pricing insight with regional or central RGM teams. Market learning, implications, and recommended actions are proactively socialized, enriched with forward-looking insight, and used to shape wider decisions.

questions.text: The market maintains a 3–5 year pricing direction that anticipates inflation, affordability pressure, competitive moves, and structural channel change.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide 2–5 year roadmap, scenario plan, profit pool work, or future-oriented business case.

anchors.1: No meaningful forward-looking view exists for 3–5 year pricing direction. Inflation, affordability pressure, competitive moves, and structural channel change are not used to shape medium- to long-term choices.
anchors.2: An initial forward-looking view exists for 3–5 year pricing direction, but inflation, affordability pressure, competitive moves, and structural channel change are high level, weakly evidenced, or not yet linked to action.
anchors.3: A workable forward-looking view exists for 3–5 year pricing direction. Inflation, affordability pressure, competitive moves, and structural channel change inform planning for major priorities, but scenario depth and strategic linkage are limited.
anchors.4: A strong forward-looking view exists for 3–5 year pricing direction. Inflation, affordability pressure, competitive moves, and structural channel change are evidence-based and actively shape medium-term planning and investment choices.
anchors.5: A leading forward-looking view exists for 3–5 year pricing direction. Inflation, affordability pressure, competitive moves, and structural channel change are scenario-tested, dynamic, and directly shape current decisions as well as 3–5 year direction.

questions.text: Pricing is used proactively to shape future value creation, not only to recover cost pressure.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide 2–5 year roadmap, scenario plan, profit pool work, or future-oriented business case.

anchors.1: No meaningful forward-looking view exists for pricing as a value-creation lever. Profit growth, portfolio shaping, and long-term strategic trade-offs are not used to shape medium- to long-term choices.
anchors.2: An initial forward-looking view exists for pricing as a value-creation lever, but profit growth, portfolio shaping, and long-term strategic trade-offs are high level, weakly evidenced, or not yet linked to action.
anchors.3: A workable forward-looking view exists for pricing as a value-creation lever. Profit growth, portfolio shaping, and long-term strategic trade-offs inform planning for major priorities, but scenario depth and strategic linkage are limited.
anchors.4: A strong forward-looking view exists for pricing as a value-creation lever. Profit growth, portfolio shaping, and long-term strategic trade-offs are evidence-based and actively shape medium-term planning and investment choices.
anchors.5: A leading forward-looking view exists for pricing as a value-creation lever. Profit growth, portfolio shaping, and long-term strategic trade-offs are scenario-tested, dynamic, and directly shape current decisions as well as 3–5 year direction.

questions.text: The market uses pack-level performance, shopper behavior, and financial metrics to evaluate whether pack roles are delivering intended outcomes.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide dashboard, model output, analysis file, KPI tracker, or tool screenshot.

anchors.1: No reliable capability exists to evaluate whether pack roles are delivering. Decision support around pack-level performance, shopper behavior, and financial metrics is absent, too weak, or not decision-grade.
anchors.2: Basic capability exists to evaluate whether pack roles are delivering, but decision support around pack-level performance, shopper behavior, and financial metrics is limited in scope, mostly manual, or used only occasionally.
anchors.3: A workable capability exists to evaluate whether pack roles are delivering for major decisions, and decision support around pack-level performance, shopper behavior, and financial metrics provides useful directional input, but depth and consistency remain limited.
anchors.4: A strong capability exists to evaluate whether pack roles are delivering. Decision support around pack-level performance, shopper behavior, and financial metrics is robust, regularly updated, and actively used in decision making.
anchors.5: A leading capability exists to evaluate whether pack roles are delivering. Decision support around pack-level performance, shopper behavior, and financial metrics is integrated, predictive where relevant, and used to continuously improve strategic and financial decisions.

questions.text: The market can identify where value is created or destroyed across the pack-price ladder.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide dashboard, model output, analysis file, KPI tracker, or tool screenshot.

anchors.1: No reliable capability exists to diagnose value creation or destruction across the pack-price ladder. Decision support around price-pack gaps, role performance, and ladder economics is absent, too weak, or not decision-grade.
anchors.2: Basic capability exists to diagnose value creation or destruction across the pack-price ladder, but decision support around price-pack gaps, role performance, and ladder economics is limited in scope, mostly manual, or used only occasionally.
anchors.3: A workable capability exists to diagnose value creation or destruction across the pack-price ladder for major decisions, and decision support around price-pack gaps, role performance, and ladder economics provides useful directional input, but depth and consistency remain limited.
anchors.4: A strong capability exists to diagnose value creation or destruction across the pack-price ladder. Decision support around price-pack gaps, role performance, and ladder economics is robust, regularly updated, and actively used in decision making.
anchors.5: A leading capability exists to diagnose value creation or destruction across the pack-price ladder. Decision support around price-pack gaps, role performance, and ladder economics is integrated, predictive where relevant, and used to continuously improve strategic and financial decisions.

questions.text: The market has a clear localized OBPPC strategy linked to shopper occasions, channel realities, and category growth priorities.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide a current strategy deck, roadmap, architecture, or decision memo.

anchors.1: No clear localized OBPPC strategy is in place. The business lacks structure around shopper occasions, channel realities, and category growth priorities, so choices remain reactive and weakly aligned to strategy.
anchors.2: A basic localized OBPPC strategy is in place in selected parts of the business, but the treatment of shopper occasions, channel realities, and category growth priorities is incomplete, outdated, or inconsistently applied.
anchors.3: A defined localized OBPPC strategy is in place for major brands, channels, customers, or packs, and shopper occasions, channel realities, and category growth priorities inform key decisions, but coverage and rigor remain uneven.
anchors.4: A strong localized OBPPC strategy is documented and regularly used. Shopper occasions, channel realities, and category growth priorities are clearly translated into decisions through an insight-led process.
anchors.5: A leading localized OBPPC strategy is dynamic and forward-looking. Shopper occasions, channel realities, and category growth priorities are continuously refreshed and actively shape both current choices and medium- to long-term value creation.

questions.text: Key packs have clearly defined roles such as recruitment, core, premium, immediate consumption, or future consumption.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide a current strategy deck, roadmap, architecture, or decision memo.

anchors.1: No clear pack-role architecture is in place. The business lacks structure around recruitment, core, premium, immediate-consumption, and future-consumption roles, so choices remain reactive and weakly aligned to strategy.
anchors.2: A basic pack-role architecture is in place in selected parts of the business, but the treatment of recruitment, core, premium, immediate-consumption, and future-consumption roles is incomplete, outdated, or inconsistently applied.
anchors.3: A defined pack-role architecture is in place for major brands, channels, customers, or packs, and recruitment, core, premium, immediate-consumption, and future-consumption roles inform key decisions, but coverage and rigor remain uneven.
anchors.4: A strong pack-role architecture is documented and regularly used. Recruitment, core, premium, immediate-consumption, and future-consumption roles are clearly translated into decisions through an insight-led process.
anchors.5: A leading pack-role architecture is dynamic and forward-looking. Recruitment, core, premium, immediate-consumption, and future-consumption roles are continuously refreshed and actively shape both current choices and medium- to long-term value creation.

questions.text: The price-pack architecture is designed intentionally to support both recruitment and premiumization without internal overlap.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide a current strategy deck, roadmap, architecture, or decision memo.

anchors.1: No clear price-pack architecture is in place. The business lacks structure around recruitment logic, premiumization logic, and avoidance of internal overlap, so choices remain reactive and weakly aligned to strategy.
anchors.2: A basic price-pack architecture is in place in selected parts of the business, but the treatment of recruitment logic, premiumization logic, and avoidance of internal overlap is incomplete, outdated, or inconsistently applied.
anchors.3: A defined price-pack architecture is in place for major brands, channels, customers, or packs, and recruitment logic, premiumization logic, and avoidance of internal overlap inform key decisions, but coverage and rigor remain uneven.
anchors.4: A strong price-pack architecture is documented and regularly used. Recruitment logic, premiumization logic, and avoidance of internal overlap are clearly translated into decisions through an insight-led process.
anchors.5: A leading price-pack architecture is dynamic and forward-looking. Recruitment logic, premiumization logic, and avoidance of internal overlap are continuously refreshed and actively shape both current choices and medium- to long-term value creation.

questions.text: There is a regular process to review pack-price ladder health, pack gaps, role conflicts, and SKU rationalization opportunities.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide SOP, governance routine, review deck, workflow, or implementation example.

anchors.1: No reliable process exists for review pack-price ladder health. The business handles pack gaps, role conflicts, and rationalization opportunities ad hoc, weakly controls them, or does not follow through.
anchors.2: A basic process exists for review pack-price ladder health, but the management of pack gaps, role conflicts, and rationalization opportunities is manual, inconsistent, or applied only in selected cases.
anchors.3: A defined process exists for review pack-price ladder health in major cases, and pack gaps, role conflicts, and rationalization opportunities are reviewed periodically, but discipline and closed-loop follow-through are uneven.
anchors.4: A robust process exists for review pack-price ladder health. Pack gaps, role conflicts, and rationalization opportunities are reviewed regularly, exceptions are managed, and actions are followed through.
anchors.5: A leading process exists for review pack-price ladder health. Pack gaps, role conflicts, and rationalization opportunities are tightly governed, rapidly escalated when needed, and continuously optimized through closed-loop management.

questions.text: OBPPC decisions are translated into channel-specific action plans and tracked in execution.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide SOP, governance routine, review deck, workflow, or implementation example.

anchors.1: No reliable process exists for translate OBPPC decisions into channel action plans. The business handles channel-specific execution, tracking, and follow-through ad hoc, weakly controls them, or does not follow through.
anchors.2: A basic process exists for translate OBPPC decisions into channel action plans, but the management of channel-specific execution, tracking, and follow-through is manual, inconsistent, or applied only in selected cases.
anchors.3: A defined process exists for translate OBPPC decisions into channel action plans in major cases, and channel-specific execution, tracking, and follow-through are reviewed periodically, but discipline and closed-loop follow-through are uneven.
anchors.4: A robust process exists for translate OBPPC decisions into channel action plans. Channel-specific execution, tracking, and follow-through are reviewed regularly, exceptions are managed, and actions are followed through.
anchors.5: A leading process exists for translate OBPPC decisions into channel action plans. Channel-specific execution, tracking, and follow-through are tightly governed, rapidly escalated when needed, and continuously optimized through closed-loop management.

questions.text: Monitoring tools or dashboards are used to track OBPPC implementation and outcomes.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide dashboard, model output, analysis file, KPI tracker, or tool screenshot.

anchors.1: No effective tools are used for OBPPC implementation monitoring. Visibility into pack-role execution, performance trends, and outcome tracking is unreliable, late, or absent.
anchors.2: Basic tools exist for OBPPC implementation monitoring, but pack-role execution, performance trends, and outcome tracking are tracked manually, inconsistently, or only for selected items.
anchors.3: Useful tools support OBPPC implementation monitoring for major brands, channels, customers, or packs, and pack-role execution, performance trends, and outcome tracking are tracked regularly, but insight-to-action remains uneven.
anchors.4: Strong tools support OBPPC implementation monitoring. Pack-role execution, performance trends, and outcome tracking are monitored through regular dashboards or alerts and are actively used to manage performance.
anchors.5: Leading tools support OBPPC implementation monitoring. Pack-role execution, performance trends, and outcome tracking are automated, timely, and embedded in workflows to enable rapid intervention and ongoing optimization.

questions.text: OBPPC strategy is jointly owned by RGM, Marketing, Sales, and Supply / Commercial Planning where relevant.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide meeting cadence, RACI, forum minutes, or cross-functional decision example.

anchors.1: No clear cross-functional governance exists for joint ownership of OBPPC strategy. Management of alignment across RGM, Marketing, Sales, and Supply or Commercial Planning depends largely on individual relationships or single-function decisions.
anchors.2: Basic cross-functional governance exists for joint ownership of OBPPC strategy, but management of alignment across RGM, Marketing, Sales, and Supply or Commercial Planning is irregular, narrow in participation, or weak in decision authority.
anchors.3: A defined governance process exists for joint ownership of OBPPC strategy, and alignment across RGM, Marketing, Sales, and Supply or Commercial Planning are discussed in major decisions, but participation and follow-through are uneven.
anchors.4: Strong cross-functional governance exists for joint ownership of OBPPC strategy. Alignment across rgm, marketing, sales, and supply or commercial planning are reviewed through clear forums, ownership, and escalation routes.
anchors.5: Leading cross-functional governance exists for joint ownership of OBPPC strategy. Alignment across rgm, marketing, sales, and supply or commercial planning are managed through high-quality dialogue, clear trade-offs, and rapid aligned decisions.

questions.text: Market OBPPC insights are systematically shared across functions and with regional stakeholders.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide meeting cadence, RACI, forum minutes, or cross-functional decision example.

anchors.1: No systematic sharing exists for sharing OBPPC insight across functions and with regional stakeholders. Portfolio learning, pack-role implications, and recommended actions are retained locally, fragmented, or shared only on request.
anchors.2: Basic sharing exists for sharing OBPPC insight across functions and with regional stakeholders, but portfolio learning, pack-role implications, and recommended actions are shared irregularly, at a high level, or without enough context to drive action.
anchors.3: A defined sharing routine exists for sharing OBPPC insight across functions and with regional stakeholders, and portfolio learning, pack-role implications, and recommended actions are circulated on important topics, but sharing is not yet proactive or consistent.
anchors.4: Strong sharing exists for sharing OBPPC insight across functions and with regional stakeholders. Portfolio learning, pack-role implications, and recommended actions are communicated regularly with enough context to support alignment and action.
anchors.5: Leading sharing exists for sharing OBPPC insight across functions and with regional stakeholders. Portfolio learning, pack-role implications, and recommended actions are proactively socialized, enriched with forward-looking insight, and used to shape wider decisions.

questions.text: The market has a medium- to long-term view of how the pack-price architecture should evolve based on macro and shopper change.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide 2–5 year roadmap, scenario plan, profit pool work, or future-oriented business case.

anchors.1: No meaningful forward-looking view exists for medium- to long-term evolution of the pack-price architecture. Macro change, shopper change, and architecture implications are not used to shape medium- to long-term choices.
anchors.2: An initial forward-looking view exists for medium- to long-term evolution of the pack-price architecture, but macro change, shopper change, and architecture implications are high level, weakly evidenced, or not yet linked to action.
anchors.3: A workable forward-looking view exists for medium- to long-term evolution of the pack-price architecture. Macro change, shopper change, and architecture implications inform planning for major priorities, but scenario depth and strategic linkage are limited.
anchors.4: A strong forward-looking view exists for medium- to long-term evolution of the pack-price architecture. Macro change, shopper change, and architecture implications are evidence-based and actively shape medium-term planning and investment choices.
anchors.5: A leading forward-looking view exists for medium- to long-term evolution of the pack-price architecture. Macro change, shopper change, and architecture implications are scenario-tested, dynamic, and directly shape current decisions as well as 3–5 year direction.

questions.text: The market can identify future growth corridors and whitespace opportunities within the pack-price ladder.
questions.evidencePrompt: Provide 2–5 year roadmap, scenario plan, profit pool work, or future-oriented business case.

anchors.1: No meaningful forward-looking view exists for future growth corridors and whitespace opportunities in the pack-price ladder. Unserved needs, ladder gaps, and future pack-price opportunities are not used to shape medium- to long-term choices.
anchors.2: An initial forward-looking view exists for future growth corridors and whitespace opportunities in the pack-price ladder, but unserved needs, ladder gaps, and future pack-price opportunities are high level, weakly evidenced, or not yet linked to action.
